commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=098bd97f7c3b7cef292e9d2733a0ed36ed0c13a0)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds Windows TTS provider fallback, a new `tts.status` readiness command, fallback-aware speak results, setup/MCP/CLI docs updates, and regression tests.

Reproducibility: yes. for the review finding. Source inspection shows the PR head falls back to Windows for explicit non-ready non-Windows providers, while current main and v0.6.3 dispatch to provider-specific failures; no live audio run was performed in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Manual review and contributor action are needed because the remaining blockers are provider-contract/product judgment and real behavior proof, not a safe automated repair lane.

target.title: Add TTS provider fallback and tts.status readiness command
target.body:
## Summary

Brings the Windows node's text-to-speech (TTS) closer to a first-class experience by making provider selection resilient and observable. Two behavior gaps are closed plus a stale-doc cleanup. Scoped to the service/capability layer — no chat UI changes.

## What & why

Windows TTS supports three providers — **Piper** (local neural, default), **Windows** (built-in OS speech, always available), and **ElevenLabs** (cloud).

**1. Graceful provider fallback.** Previously, if the requested/configured provider wasn't ready (no ElevenLabs API key, or the Piper voice not downloaded), `tts.speak` **threw** and nothing was spoken — even though the always-available Windows voice could have served the request. `SpeakAsync` now resolves an *effective* provider and falls back to Windows TTS instead of failing. The result reports `provider` (who actually spoke), `requestedProvider`, and `fellBack`.
- On fallback, the per-call `voiceId`/`model` are dropped so the Windows synthesizer doesn't reject a Piper/ElevenLabs voice id.
- A stale/removed **configured** Windows voice now degrades to the system default voice rather than throwing, so the fallback always speaks. An **explicit per-call** Windows voice that's invalid still throws (genuine caller error).

**2. New `tts.status` command.** A read-only readiness probe: reports `configuredProvider`, `effectiveProvider` (after fallback), `willFallBack`, and per-provider readiness (`ready | needs-api-key | needs-voice | voice-not-downloaded | unavailable`). PII-free — never echoes voice ids, API key fragments, or device names. Lets callers/UI know which providers are usable before attempting playback.

**3. Stale docstring fix.** `PiperVoiceManager` carried a `TODO (pre-GA): SHA-256 verification` comment, but tarball verification is already implemented for Whisper/Piper/Silero and enforced by `AssetHashPinningTests`. Removed the misleading TODO.

## Files

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `Shared/Capabilities/TtsCapability.cs` | `StatusCommand`, `ResolveEffectiveProvider` pure helper, readiness constants, `TtsProviderResolution`/`TtsProviderStatus`/`TtsStatusResult`, `RequestedProvider`/`FellBack` on result, status handler |
| `Tray.WinUI/.../TextToSpeech/TextToSpeechService.cs` | Fallback in `SpeakAsync`, `GetProviderReadiness`, `BuildReadyProviderSet`, `GetStatus`, stale-configured-voice handling |
| `Tray.WinUI/Services/NodeService.cs` | `OnTtsStatusAsync` wiring |
| `Shared/Models.cs` | `tts.status` in `DangerousCommands` (gated by `NodeTtsEnabled`) |
| `Shared/Mcp/McpToolBridge.cs`, `WinNode.Cli/skill.md` | Command docs (kept in sync; enforced by drift test) |
| `SetupEngine/SetupContext.cs` | Export `tts.status` in `gateway.nodes.allowCommands` |
| `Shared/Audio/PiperVoiceManager.cs` | Docstring fix |
| tests | `CapabilityTests`, `ModelsTests`, `SetupConfigTests` |

## Behavior

- `tts.speak` no longer hard-fails on an unconfigured provider — it speaks (Windows) and reports `fellBack: true`.
- `tts.status` is a new read-only, PII-free readiness probe, gated behind `NodeTtsEnabled` (off by default).
- No UI changes; the Voice Settings page is untouched.

## How to verify

- **Resolver logic:** `TtsCapability.ResolveEffectiveProvider` — preferred provider wins when ready, else Windows, else preferred unchanged. Truth table in `CapabilityTests.ResolveEffectiveProvider_FallsBackToWindows`.
- **Readiness logic:** `TextToSpeechService.GetProviderReadiness` — Windows always ready; ElevenLabs needs key→voice; Piper needs a downloaded voice.
- **Targeted tests:** `dotnet test tests/OpenClaw.Shared.Tests --filter TtsCapabilityTests`.
- **Doc/registry consistency:** `dotnet test tests/OpenClaw.WinNode.Cli.Tests` covers the skill.md ↔ capability-registry drift check.
- **Live smoke** (requires `NodeTtsEnabled`): call `tts.status` → expect `windows` `ready`; set provider to `elevenlabs` with no key, then `tts.speak` → expect an audible Windows voice and `fellBack: true`.

## Validation

- `./build.ps1` — all projects build.
- `Shared.Tests`, `Tray.Tests`, `WinNode.Cli.Tests` (incl. skill.md drift), `SetupEngine.Tests` — all pass.

## Risks / limitations

- **Silent degradation on an explicit per-call provider:** an explicit `provider:"piper"` with an undownloaded voice now yields a Windows voice rather than an actionable error. Intentional (goal: don't fail); the information is preserved in `fellBack` / `requestedProvider` / `tts.status`, so a caller that inspects them can surface "using Windows voice" to the user.
- **`willFallBack` is a configured-defaults snapshot** — a `tts.speak` call supplying its own `voiceId` may avoid a fallback the snapshot predicted. Documented on the type and in skill.md.
- **`tts.status` classification:** grouped with the other privacy-gated voice commands behind `NodeTtsEnabled`. A "safe" classification (like `device.status`) would also be defensible since it's read-only and PII-free.

Low blast radius: an additive command plus a fallback that only triggers on the path that previously threw; no schema/storage/connection changes.
target.author: bkudiess
